cards.title: Cache uvx tools in GitHub Actions with one env var
cards.headline:
Stop re-downloading
PyPI tools on every
Actions run

steps.label: Pin a cutoff date
steps.detail: Set this env var at the top of your workflow. Every uvx invocation will resolve to the newest package versions available as of that date, making resolution deterministic.

steps.label: Use the date in your cache key
steps.detail: Tying the cache key to UV_EXCLUDE_NEWER means the cached wheel set is valid as long as the date stays the same — no stale hits, no surprise upgrades.

steps.label: Bust cache by bumping the date
steps.detail: When you want newer tool versions, update UV_EXCLUDE_NEWER to today's date. The old cache is abandoned and a fresh one is populated on the next run.

steps.label: Why this beats the default
steps.detail: Without this, every workflow run hits PyPI to fetch the latest tool and dependencies, adding latency and flakiness. The astral-sh/setup-uv repo has an open issue to make caching the default behaviour.

cards.title: DOOMQL: Doom clone where SQLite is the game engine
cards.caption: Peter Gostev used GPT-5.6 Sol to build a Doom-like game where SQLite handles movement, collision, enemies, combat, and every rendered pixel — via a recursive CTE ray tracer.

script.text: cd /tmp && git clone https://github.com/petergpt/doomql && cd doomql
[DOOMQL] SQLite engine initialised. Frame loop running...
[datasette-apps] Plugin loaded. Paste app code at /-/apps

cards.title: Microchip makes MPLAB ML Dev Suite free for all

panels.head: Microchip drops the paywall on its embedded ML toolchain
panels.sub: As of July 2026, the MPLAB XC Pro Compilers and MPLAB Machine Learning Development Suite are free — unlimited installs, no licence fees.

panels.head: These tools sit between your model and the microcontroller
panels.sub: The ML Development Suite handles the workflow from training data to optimised firmware, while the XC Pro compilers apply advanced code-size and speed optimisations that were previously paywalled.

panels.head: Teams can now scale installs without per-seat cost
panels.sub: Unlimited installs means CI pipelines, shared lab benches, and contractor setups all get the same Pro-tier compiler performance that was previously restricted to paid seats.

panels.head: Lower friction for ML at the edge of regulated hardware
panels.sub: For PMs shipping vision or sensor-fusion features on PIC or AVR MCUs — medical devices, industrial monitors, smart meters — the cost barrier to Pro-grade optimisation just disappeared.

panels.head: Audit your embedded ML stack costs today
panels.sub: If your team is paying for MPLAB XC licences or deferring ML experiments because of toolchain cost, those constraints are now gone — worth a line item review this sprint.

cards.title: Coding agents spike one dev's commit graph

panels.head: One commit graph, one very visible inflection point
panels.sub: Simon Willison pulled GitHub's code-frequency chart for Datasette looking for a concrete signal of how much coding agents have changed his output. He found one.

panels.head: Datasette is a long-running, well-maintained OSS project
panels.sub: Because the project has years of baseline commit history, any sudden change in velocity stands out clearly against the noise — making it a reasonable personal benchmark.

panels.head: A sharp spike at the tail end of the chart
panels.sub: The surge in code-change frequency lines up with the arrival of Opus 4.8, GPT-5.5, Fable 5, and GPT-5.6 Sol — a cluster of frontier model releases that raised agentic coding capability noticeably.

panels.head: Personal commit graphs are now productivity instruments
panels.sub: For a solo maintainer, a chart like this is a low-effort way to see whether a new model class is actually moving the needle — no benchmark suite required.

panels.head: Model generation matters more than marginal tuning
panels.sub: The spike isn't gradual — it's abrupt, suggesting step-change model capability (not workflow tweaks) drove the output jump. PMs evaluating AI tooling upgrades should watch for similar discontinuities in their own teams' output metrics.

cards.title: AI agents should never be the DRI

panels.head: A 1979 IBM slide still settles the debate
panels.sub: "A computer can never be held accountable, therefore a computer must never make a management decision." Forty-seven years later, LLM agents haven't changed that logic.

panels.head: What a DRI actually means
panels.sub: Directly Responsible Individual — a term Apple coined and GitLab codified — names the single human who is ultimately accountable when a project succeeds or fails. One name, full ownership.

panels.head: Accountability requires a person, not a process
panels.sub: Agents can execute tasks and make micro-decisions, but they cannot own consequences. When something goes wrong, there must be a human whose job, reputation, or freedom is on the line.

panels.head: Org charts with agent DRIs are liability traps
panels.sub: In regulated domains — medical devices, autonomous vehicles, financial systems — assigning accountability to a model rather than a person creates an audit black hole and likely violates compliance requirements.

panels.head: Design every agentic workflow with a named human DRI
panels.sub: Before deploying an agent, answer: who signs off on its outputs, who gets paged when it fails, and who explains the outcome to a regulator? If you can't name that person, the system isn't ready.

cards.title: shot-scraper 1.11: three small but useful upgrades
cards.headline:
shot-scraper 1.11 makes
script-driven scraping
a little less brittle

steps.label: Slower servers no longer break video/multi
steps.detail: The server: launch mechanism now polls for port availability for up to 30 seconds instead of assuming the server is ready after a fixed 1-second wait. Useful when your local dev server is slow to boot.

steps.label: Load JS from a file, stdin, or a GitHub Gist
steps.detail: The shot-scraper, pdf, html, accessibility, and har commands all gain --js-file as an alternative to passing a raw --javascript string. The multi command gets the equivalent js_file: YAML key.

steps.label: --timeout added to javascript and html commands
steps.detail: These two commands previously lacked --timeout, breaking consistency with the rest of the CLI. Now all major sub-commands share the same option surface.
